title: Relative clauses: who, which, that, whose, where, when
lesson: Defining relative clauses tell us which person or thing we mean. Use 'who' for people, 'which' for things, and 'that' for either. Use 'whose' to show possession, 'where' for places, and 'when' for times. These clauses are essential to the meaning, so they take no commas.

items.text: That's the woman {1} won the competition.
items.explanation: Use 'who' for a person who does the action (the subject of the clause).

items.text: This is the book {1} changed my life.
items.explanation: Use 'which' for a thing. ('That' would also be correct here.)

items.text: She's the doctor {1} treated my father.
items.explanation: 'Who' refers to a person, the doctor, who performs the action of the clause.

items.text: I live in a town {1} nothing exciting ever happens.
items.explanation: Use 'where' to refer to a place; it replaces 'in which' (in the town).

items.text: Do you remember the day {1} we first met?
items.explanation: Use 'when' to refer to a time, such as 'the day'.

items.text: He's the man {1} car was stolen.
items.explanation: Use 'whose' to show possession: the car belongs to the man.

items.text: The film {1} we watched last night was boring.
items.explanation: For a thing that is the object of the clause, use 'that' (or 'which'); 'who' is only for people.

items.text: Students {1} finish early can leave the room.
items.explanation: Use 'who' for people; the students are the subject of 'finish early'. ('That' is also possible.)

items.text: This is the restaurant {1} they got engaged.
items.explanation: Use 'where' for a place (the restaurant); it means 'in which'.

items.text: I have a friend {1} brother is a famous singer.
items.explanation: Use 'whose' to show that the brother belongs to (relates to) the friend.

items.text: The keys {1} are on the table belong to me.
items.explanation: Use 'which' (or 'that') for things; 'keys' is the subject of 'are on the table'.

items.text: Winter is the season {1} I feel most tired.
items.explanation: Use 'when' to refer to a time, such as 'the season'.

items.text: My grandmother, {1} is ninety, still goes swimming every morning.

blanks.hint: non-defining clause about a person; 'that' is impossible here

items.explanation: In a non-defining clause (set off by commas), 'that' cannot be used; for a person we must use 'who'.

items.text: Paris, {1} I lived for two years, is a wonderful city.
items.explanation: Use 'where' to refer to a place (Paris); it means 'in which'. In this non-defining clause 'that' is also impossible.

items.text: My neighbour, {1} dog barks all night, has finally apologised.

blanks.hint: shows possession in a non-defining clause

items.explanation: Use 'whose' to show possession: the dog belongs to the neighbour. Only 'whose' fits this slot.

items.text: Nobody knows the reason {1} he left so suddenly.
items.explanation: Use 'why' as the relative after 'the reason'; it means 'for which'. 'Which' and 'where' don't fit this slot.

items.text: She works for a small company {1} office is in the city centre.
items.explanation: 'Whose' can refer to things as well as people: the office belongs to the company.

items.text: This is the company {1} which she works.

blanks.hint: preposition that goes with the verb 'work'

items.explanation: With preposition + 'which', the preposition is the one the verb requires: we 'work for' a company, so 'for which'.

items.text: He passed his driving test, {1} delighted his parents.

blanks.hint: non-defining; refers to the whole previous clause

items.explanation: Use 'which' (not 'that' or 'what') to refer back to a whole clause; here it means 'and this fact delighted his parents'.

items.text: The shop {1} I bought this jacket has closed down.
items.explanation: Use 'where' for a place (the shop); it means 'at which'. 'Which' alone can't follow without a preposition here.

items.text: The old harbour, {1} was rebuilt after the storm, attracts thousands of tourists.

blanks.hint: non-defining clause about a thing; 'that' is impossible here

items.explanation: In a non-defining clause (set off by commas) 'that' cannot be used; for a thing we must use 'which'.

items.text: The colleague to {1} I gave the report has since retired.

blanks.hint: object of the preposition 'to', formal register

items.explanation: Directly after a preposition (to), a person is referred to with 'whom' in formal English: 'to whom I gave the report'.
